Text: Played viola in Prague's Provisional Theatre orchestra through the 1860s — from 1866 under Smetana — leaving in 1871 to compose full-time.
Commentary: Years inside the texture as an inner-voice player.
Why Interesting: Roots his quartet writing in a violist's ear.

Text: His Austrian State Stipendium entries won over jury member Brahms, who recommended him to the publisher Simrock — the break that made his name.
Commentary: The Brahms link, one tab over in the festival week.

Text: He labeled his 1861 String Quintet 'Op. 1' and his 1862 First Quartet 'Op. 2', though Burghauser lists five earlier works (B. 6, B. 7).
Commentary: An opus tangle in the Boccherini/Gérard spirit.

Text: The story that he first trained as a butcher rests on an apprenticeship certificate Burghauser exposed as a forgery in 1987.
He often closed a finished manuscript with a line of thanks to God — 'Bohu díky!'
Commentary: A human detail at the foot of the score.

Text: Symphony No. 9, 'From the New World', was a New York Philharmonic commission, premiered under Anton Seidl in 1893.
Commentary: The headline fact, with the conductor named.

Text: His daughter Otýlie married his pupil Josef Suk in 1898; Suk was second violinist of the Bohemian Quartet.
Commentary: A direct line into the string-quartet world.

Text: Michael Beckerman has argued Dvořák suffered from agoraphobia — a proposal that remains debated.
He spent the summer of 1893 — four months — in the Czech farming community of Spillville, Iowa.
Commentary: The Spillville retreat behind the 'American' Quartet, in his own words.
Why Interesting: Origin of a famous chamber work.

Text: He published three pieces for the US press: 'The Real Value of Negro Melodies' (NY Herald, 1893), 'Franz Schubert' (Century, 1894), 'Music in America' (Harper's, 1895).
Commentary: Where his documented public statements live.
Source: Beckerman; Tibbetts, ed., Dvořák in America

Text: At Thurber's National Conservatory teaching was free to talented students; he judged a composition competition with prizes up to $1000.
Commentary: The institutional setting of his American years.
Source: Šourek, Letters and Reminiscences (letter from New York)

Text: In the Negro melodies of America I discover all that is needed for a great and noble school of music.
Commentary: His most-quoted public statement; documented, if weighty.
Why Interesting: Use with care — heavy for a light morning note.
Source: New York Herald, interview by James Creelman, 21 May 1893

Text: I look at the first page and can tell straight away whether it is the work of a dilettante or an artist.
Commentary: From a letter written from New York while judging the Conservatory competition.
Why Interesting: His confidence as a judge of talent.

Text: Forgive me for lacking a little in modesty, but I am only telling you what the American papers are constantly writing.
Commentary: Self-aware humor about the American hype around him.

Text: You know very well that I love my country above all else and am glad to offer my humble services where they are needed and wanted.
Commentary: From letter 170 to Dr. Tragy, renewing his US contract.
Source: Šourek (letter to J. Tragy, New York, 16 Nov 1893)

Text: It is an attention I am due my countrymen to make it possible for them to sing my songs in the Czech language.
Commentary: Asking Simrock to print Czech texts in his editions.
Why Interesting: His insistence on Czech, not just German.

Commentary: His pronouncement to a pupil, gazing out at the sky.
Why Interesting: Charming — and now actually sourced, not folklore.
Source: Šourek, recalled in Josef Michl, 'From Dvořák's School'
